title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
thinking_questions: Water at i = min(max_left, max_right) - height[i]. How do you know both maxes efficiently?
You could precompute left_max[] and right_max[] arrays — what's that approach's complexity?
Can you compute both maxes simultaneously with two pointers, avoiding those arrays?

hints.L1: At each position, water = min(tallest to the left, tallest to the right) - height[i]. Think about efficiently knowing both maxes.
hints.L2: Two-pointer approach: maintain left_max and right_max. Process from the side with the smaller max — that side's water level is fully determined.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: process smaller-max side first — it's the bottleneck
left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
left_max, right_max = 0, 0
water = 0
while left < right:
    if left_max <= right_max:
        left_max = max(left_max, height[left])
        water += left_max - height[left]  # left_max is the limiting factor
        left += 1
    else:
        right_max = max(right_max, height[right])
        water += right_max - height[right]
        right -= 1
return water
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)
# Why safe: when left_max≤right_max, water at left is exactly left_max-height[left]
# because right_max ≥ left_max, so left_max is the min

follow_up_questions: When left_max≤right_max, why is the water at left guaranteed to be left_max-height[left]?
Walk through the precompute arrays approach then explain how two pointers reduce space.
How would the monotonic stack approach work? When might you prefer it?
solution_notes: Two-pointer: process smaller-max side. O(n), O(1). Key: smaller max is the water ceiling; other side ≥ it.
